error: He grew distant, and I knew he’d been thinking about divorce for weeks and had decided to wait to tell me until he got home.
erroneous_event: In the week that followed, he barely checked in. When he went golfing without a word, I snapped and texted how lonely and angry I felt. He reacted poorly. We were both raw, half a world apart. <error>He grew distant, and I knew he’d been thinking about divorce for weeks and had decided to wait to tell me until he got home.</error>
story:
On New Year’s Day 2023, I was in Iowa, in our cute Folk Victorian, “happily-ish” married with two dogs. My husband, Eric, was deployed and due home in about a month. I counted the days.

Ten days before his return, our senior pit bull mix, Bailey, died. It wasn’t unexpected, but it was sudden. I rushed her to the vet and called Eric over and over from that cold, sterile room. When he finally picked up, he said goodbye over FaceTime while I held her as she passed. It broke us both, but I was the one there. I grieved alone; he buried himself in work.

In the week that followed, he barely checked in. When he went golfing without a word, I snapped and texted how lonely and angry I felt. He reacted poorly. We were both raw, half a world apart. He grew distant, and I knew he’d been thinking about divorce for weeks and had decided to wait to tell me until he got home. On FaceTime, I asked outright. He didn’t want to say—he wanted to wait until he got home—but he admitted he’d been thinking about divorce.

It wasn’t about that one text. It was about the life. About months of forced separation every year. About living far from family and friends because the military said so. About years of “How can we make each other feel seen?” that never quite worked. We loved and respected each other, but we lacked emotional intimacy. Things were always fine, but rarely great.

“Let’s go to couples therapy,” I said. We did—first over video, then in person when he returned in mid-February, near our anniversary. It was too late. Instead of the reunion I’d imagined, the house felt heavy and awkward.

Have you seen the glass-shattering episode of How I Met Your Mother? Once someone points out the thing, you can’t unsee it. After he floated divorce—days after Bailey died—the ceiling shattered. I realized I’d been settling. I kept telling myself we just had to make it four more years and we’d finally get the life we dreamed of. In reality, we were roommates-with-benefits who got along well enough.

I always respected his service. But his deployment wasn’t a foxhole; it was a billet on a tropical island, with plenty of time for golf and snorkeling. He’d said more than once he’d deploy all the time if it weren’t for me. Meanwhile, I was surviving a subzero Iowa winter in a drafty old house, handling a car accident and a broken hand, and spending Thanksgiving, New Year’s, and my thirtieth birthday alone. I had given up living near my support system, reshaped my career around his schedule, and accepted a nontraditional marriage. I chose him, but my life was ruled by his career—and I wasn’t getting much back.

At the vet, as I held Bailey, he said he was relieved he wasn’t there and wondered if he should hang up. I told myself he was in shock. It never sat right.

So when he finally said the word—divorce—the Jenga tower fell. I didn’t cry. I felt free: free of a life that was only “fine,” free of a rhythm that no longer fit us. I knew I was meant for more.

Now I’m down to one perfect dog, packing to leave Iowa, and nearly legally single. I went from dreaming of his return to dreading it. I took off the rose-colored glasses. I’m scared, yes—but excited. Maybe I’ll have my own Eat Pray Love moment. Maybe he’ll find someone who fits him better. Either way, I’m ready to live my life on my terms.
